taskVersion.name: epu_q2_short
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_short")
def epu_q2_short(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, short_pre, short_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

Archi

AMERICAN CisLTisRE 'The Sum of All Fears' vs. 'American Idol.': And ... Kakutani, Michiko New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 11, 2002; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg- 35" AMERICAN CisLTisRE 'The Sum of All Fears' vs. 'American Idol' IN the uncertain days after 9/11, one of the few things asserted with absolute certainty was that our culture would be Indelibly altered. A year later, very little has changed. Irony thrives. Gratuitous film and televi­sion violence rules. And instead of a kinder, gentler, more high-minded society, we are given Anna Nicole Smith lolling about her .bedrtom, Playboy bunnies doing "Fear ^Factor" stunts, and celebrities boxing. 1 Though movies like "Collateral Damage" ;with Arnold Schwarzenegger were initially postponed, the big screen has since been :awash In blow-'em-up extravaganzas, in­cluding "The Sum of Ail Fears," a No. 1 hit 'that showed us the nuclear destruction of Baltimore. While Barney, the annoying pur­ple dinosaur, is trying to make a comeback in what many predicted would be a more family-oriented environment, prurient shows like "The Bachelor" and "Meet My Folks" have turned sexual commerce Into prime-time entertainment, and Darwinian competition and sophomorlc mean-spirited­ness continue to thrive on "Dog Eat Dog" •and "Spy TV." The glossy magazines are once again Tilled with pieces deconstructing J. Lo's wardrobe and Tom Cruise's love life; shark stories were a summer staple again on television

[...TRUNCATED...]

greeting card verse about heroic acts heal­ing "a wounded heart." '■ No doubt artistic treatments of 9/11 will grow increasingly metaphorical in the years to come. • As literature about the Holocaust, Hiro­shima and AIDS shows, the earliest respons­es to a traumatic event tend to be autobio­graphical accounts that pay witness to his­tory; the fictional works that ensue tend to evolve from somewhat didactic, explana­tory accounts into stylized ones, in which 'the tragedy is universalized, or woven into the fabric of ordinary life, as ripples from the event, rather than the event itself, as­sume center stage. • It will take time — and emotional distance — for artists to achieve perspective on Sept. 11, 2001. Tolstoy wrote "War and Peace!' some 60 years after Napoleon's invasion of Russia. Benjamin Britten's "War Re­quiem," memorializing World War 11, was not heard until 1961. And John Corigliano's "Symphony No. 1," the so-called AIDS Sym­phony, did not premiere until 1990, a decade after the composer began losing friends to the illness. In the case of last year's terrorist attacks, novelists, filmmakers and other artists are still gestating or working on projects in­formed by 9/11, trying, in Don DeLillo's words, to "understand what this day has done to us," and to give ''memory, tender­ness and meaning to all that howling space" at ground zero.
